# Questions in Livestream 246

Live Stream #246: Egyptian Royal Streaming

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=466" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:46</a> | Are they going to make the second book into an audiobook? | Probably not unless sales improve, because the first audiobook sold very poorly relative to the work involved. | He says the first novel's audiobook was distributed through many vendors, but sales were "appallingly bad" compared with the paperback, hardcover, and ebook. Because producing it took about forty hours of work, he is not closing the door permanently but says more audiobooks are not worth doing unless audiobook sales improve. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:20</a> | Can the pharaonic dynasty be traced down to the present day? | No; he says the native dynastic line ended long ago, and there is no surviving royal succession to trace forward. | He says the last native Egyptian line ended with Nectanebo, and the Ptolemaic line also ended without surviving heirs after Cleopatra's children. One could try to treat Roman emperors as a continuation, but even that line becomes disconnected after the Roman imperial shift, so he sees no meaningful surviving pharaonic dynasty to trace. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=779" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:59</a> | What temple-cultic language appears in Genesis 1-11 besides creation and the ark? | He points to image-of-God statuary themes, temple-court imagery, sacrifice, and the offerings of Cain, Abel, and Noah. | He lists the image of God as statuary language, sacred-space organization, sacrifice, clean and unclean animals, the offerings of Cain, Abel, and Noah, Noah's burnt offering, altar construction with unhewn stone, cherubim and seraphim, ritual purification, and the draining of sacrificial blood. He frames Genesis 1-11 as full of temple and cultic motifs that prepare Israel for tabernacle ritual. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=933" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:33</a> | Does the table of presence go back in concept to creation? | In one sense yes, because it is a perpetual offering and offerings themselves go back to the earliest chapters of Genesis. | He connects the table of presence to creation only in the broad sense that it is a standing presentation of an offering to God. Since Cain and Abel are the first humans shown bringing offerings, and Noah's altar also belongs to the early ritual pattern, the furniture's concept participates in that older offering logic rather than deriving from a direct creation-table institution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1042" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:22</a> | Why do none of the three most central creeds say "I believe in the Bible"? | Timing and context: complete Bible codices were not yet the iconic common object people imagine, and the creeds were aimed at disputed doctrines rather than at an authority nobody in the church was denying. | He argues that the Bible as one iconic bound book had not yet become the common object later Christians imagine, especially in the eastern setting where the creeds were formed. Churches usually had scrolls or partial collections, while creeds were written to answer live doctrinal denials about Christ and the Trinity, not to affirm scriptural authority that the church already assumed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1124" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:44</a> | What about Codex Vaticanus and Alexandrinus? | He says such manuscripts existed, but they were rare, expensive, not copies of one another, and not what ordinary Christians or churches usually had available. | He acknowledges that large biblical manuscripts existed, but says they were expensive, uncommon, held in major institutions such as monasteries, and not something a normal Christian or even a normal basilica would possess. Most churches would have scrolls of various biblical books rather than a complete Bible, so those codices do not change his point about why the creeds did not speak in later "the Bible" language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1280" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:20</a> | What is monolithic monotheism again? | He defines it as belief in one personal God without distinct divine persons, giving Islam as an example and contrasting it with monarchial monotheism. | He defines monolithic monotheism as one-person monotheism: one God manifested in only one person. Islam is his example, because it denies that Jesus and the Holy Spirit are God, whereas monarchial monotheism can have multiple divine persons and lesser spirits under the figurehead of God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1394" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:14</a> | If intelligent extraterrestrials exist, are humans supposed to expand through the universe and bring them the gospel? | He says humanity is commanded to fill the earth, not the universe, so space evangelization is not a biblical imperative. | He says Scripture commands humanity to fill the earth, not the universe, so space travel is neither forbidden nor required. If intelligent extraterrestrials existed, their spiritual state would matter: they might be unfallen, have their own provision of grace, or possibly need human witness, but no biblical mandate currently requires humans to go evangelize them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1537" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:37</a> | In Genesis, when Jacob wrestled with God, is there an interpretation that he actually wrestled with a demon? | He does not see how that reading works, apart from someone over-reading the fact that Jacob was wounded. | He says the demon interpretation probably comes from overemphasizing Jacob's injury, but the text itself works against it. Jacob asks for a blessing, the figure gives him the name Israel for striving with God and persevering, and the figure blesses him; in his view, demons do not bless, making the reading very difficult to sustain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1811" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:11</a> | Why is no one in exodus studies engaging Shlomit Bechar's Hazor work the way some engage Nigro's work? | He says some people do engage it, including him, and that Bechar's work especially undercuts the claim of a 15th-century Hazor destruction layer. | He says Bechar is being engaged and that he himself finds her work solid. The important point for Exodus debates is that she denies a real 15th-century destruction layer at Hazor; in his summary, the supposed early-date destruction is only a small, localized trace near a gate or wall, whereas Joshua's destruction would require the whole city to be burned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=2296" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:16</a> | Why is Jacob named Israel again in Genesis 35:10 after already being renamed earlier? | He treats Genesis 35 as a reaffirmation in a new setting rather than a contradictory second first-naming. | He reads Genesis 35 in the setting of Jacob going to Bethel, purifying his household, and building a cultic site. The repeated naming is probably a reiteration or reaffirmation of the Genesis 32 naming, perhaps with the final "thus he called him Israel" functioning as a scribal gloss, rather than a contradictory claim that this is the first time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=3162" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:42</a> | Could a name in Ramesses I's Amara West topographical list be Jericho as proposed by Grzybek? | He says it is possible but not very likely; the better reading probably points somewhere in Syria, and the proposal has not gained much traction. | He says Grzybek is not being foolish, but the list section and likely reading point more naturally somewhere in the Syrian or northern Canaanite sphere than to Jericho. He leaves the proposal possible in principle but says he is reticent at first blush and notes that the reading has not gained much scholarly traction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=3299" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:59</a> | Does Genesis 47:26 imply Joseph's 20% tax was still in place by Dynasty 19? | Yes; he says the verse itself points that way and notes the tax continued much later in Egyptian history as well. | He initially hesitates, then reads Genesis 47:26 and notices the phrase "to this day," concluding that the verse does indicate the fifth tax was still in force from the narrator's perspective. He adds that the tax is known to have continued even into the Third Intermediate Period, becoming a standing Egyptian tax rate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=3422" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:02</a> | Could Merneptah have indirectly taken credit for an Israelite defeat or setback without directly encountering Israel? | No. He says Egyptian victory texts were religious offerings, so a king could not simply claim a victory he had not actually performed. | He says Egyptian victory inscriptions are religious offerings, not merely propaganda in the modern sense. Because offerings to the gods operate within reciprocal economy, a king could spin a real victory but could not safely offer an outright lie; Merneptah would need to have actually performed the defeat or setback involving Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=3750" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:30</a> | Does the reference to spoked wheels in an Amenemhat II inscription show knowledge of wheeled devices in Dynasty 12? | Yes, but as imported technology from Canaan rather than a fully native Egyptian wheeled tradition at that point. | He says the inscription shows Egyptians knew about wheeled devices, but only as imported or captured technology. In his account, Middle Kingdom Egypt lacked the craft industry and environmental adaptation needed to build and use wheeled vehicles domestically, so Canaanite wagon wheels as booty are still far from Egyptian chariot technology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=3924" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:24</a> | Does 1 Peter 3:21 teach that baptism saves? | He treats the verse in the context of the gospel and argues it should not be read as making the water rite itself the saving cause. | He reads 1 Peter 3:18-21 through the Noah comparison and distinguishes "save" as preserve from "save" as initial salvation. Baptism preserves through an appeal to God for a good conscience and is a promise to live unto Christ, so he does not treat the removal of dirt by water as the saving cause. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:13</a> | Does he think Aristotle is in heaven? | He does not know; he says it would depend on Aristotle's relation to Christ, while allowing that pre-Christian people could not respond to Christ in the same explicit way. | He says Aristotle's final state depends on what he does with Christ, but Aristotle belongs to the category of people who died before hearing of Christ and therefore could not explicitly accept or reject him in the later Christian sense. He does not exclude the possibility, but says the Bible does not give enough information for confidence either way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4336" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:16</a> | What is worship? Is it just singing? | No; he defines worship as ascribing worth to God and Christ, which can include music but is broader than music. | He defines worship as ascribing worth, glory, and honor to God and Jesus Christ. Singing may do that, especially when directed to God, but other media can also be worship, including poetry, responsive reading, public prayer, instrumental music, and other acts that assign glory to God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:55</a> | Do declarative praise psalms count as worship? | Yes; he says they can count as worship because worship can include any medium that ascribes glory and honor to God. | He says declarative praise psalms absolutely count as worship because worship is not limited to singing. Responsive reading, reciting psalms, poetry, music, and even tithing can be worship when they ascribe worth, glory, and honor to God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4594" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:34</a> | Does fasting during the day while feasting at night count as worship? | Not really; he says fasting by itself is not the same thing as worship. | He answers briefly that this pattern is not really worship, while recognizing what the question is getting at. In the surrounding discussion, worship requires ascribing glory and honor to God, so the mere practice of fasting and later feasting does not automatically meet that definition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4622" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:02</a> | What will happen to tithing as worship if cash is illegal? | He says preserving the ritual aspect of giving becomes harder without cash or checks, but other tangible payment forms may still be possible. | He worries that purely automatic giving can remove the physical ritual aspect of tithing, which he considers part of its worship value. If cash and checks disappear, maintaining that tangible act becomes harder, though he mentions alternatives such as money orders or even a physical transfer of digital value as ways one might preserve a ritual act of giving. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4832" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:32</a> | Is 1 John 1:8-10 about initial justification or ongoing repentance in a Christian's life? | He reads it as the ongoing believing life: believers continue walking in the light, confessing sin, and being cleansed rather than claiming sinless perfection. | He begins at 1 John 1:6 and says the passage addresses believers who claim fellowship with Christ. Walking in the light involves ongoing fellowship and continual cleansing, so verses 8-10 reject Christian perfectionism and call believers to keep confessing sin rather than pretending they no longer sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=5227" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:07</a> | Is walking in darkness willful, deliberate sin? | It can be, but he says it can also be drift or iniquity, where a believer is distracted or misdirected and ends up walking in darkness. | He says walking in darkness can be deliberate, but it can also result from drift, distraction, or misdirection. The danger of iniquity is that someone may move off course gradually and then lie to themselves about where they are spiritually. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=5479" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:19</a> | Is walking in darkness a disruption in a believer's relationship with God that can lead to apostasy if unchecked? | Yes; he says walking in darkness cuts connection with God and requires believers to examine whether their walk matches their profession. | He agrees that walking in darkness disrupts the believer's relationship with God and can become dangerous if unaddressed. He connects this with examining oneself with fear and trembling, asking whether one's walk remains consistent with one's profession of faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=5675" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:35</a> | What other Egyptologist scholars does he recommend for reading about the Exodus besides himself, Kitchen, and Hoffmeier? | He recommends Mark Janzen as someone who has done good work, though not a large amount, on the Exodus. | He gives Mark Janzen as the main additional recommendation. He says Janzen has not written a huge amount on the Exodus, but what he has done is good work and worth reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=5723" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:23</a> | Does the Septuagint preserve the authentic version of Jeremiah? | He is cautious; he says the Septuagint preserves the version its translators had, but that does not necessarily make a shorter or longer version original. | He says Septuagint books are sometimes shorter and sometimes longer, and he suspects the translators often worked with one text or multiple similar texts and tried to preserve available readings. In Jeremiah's case, a shorter text may preserve the version they had, but that does not automatically prove it is the original or more authentic form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=5939" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:59</a> | In Genesis 11, was the tower and city of Babel destroyed or just abandoned when God scattered the people? | The text does not say; he thinks it was probably an ordinary ziggurat that may have been abandoned, left unfinished, or ruined later. | He says Genesis does not tell us whether Babel was destroyed. He thinks the tower was probably an ordinary ziggurat, a structure for worship toward heaven rather than a skyscraper into space, and it may simply have been abandoned, left unfinished, or ruined at some later time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6019" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:19</a> | Why is there debate about Babel being a ziggurat if that seems obvious? | He says Western art shaped many imaginations by depicting Babel as a massive tower rising toward heaven rather than as an ordinary ziggurat. | He says the debate persists because Western and Renaissance art trained people to imagine a massive tower reaching into heaven, often with medieval or classical architecture. In his view, ancient ziggurats were impressive for their time but not enormous by later standards, so the popular image distorts what the Genesis structure likely was. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6303" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:03</a> | Has he read *Refuting Compromise*, and how does he answer people who call him liberal for not believing Genesis days must be 24-hour periods? | He has not read it, and he says rejecting a 24-hour-day view is not liberalism because it does not deny central Christian doctrines. | He says he has not read the book. He rejects the charge of liberalism as a category error, because not holding 24-hour creation days does not deny the deity of Christ, resurrection, crucifixion, vicarious atonement, Trinity, virgin birth, or future resurrection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:24</a> | What is happening in Deuteronomy 23:28-29? | He says he cannot answer because there is no such passage in English Bibles. | He looks up the reference and says Deuteronomy 23 does not have verses 28-29 in English Bibles. Because the cited passage does not exist in that form, he cannot explain what is happening there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6543" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:03</a> | In dating the Exodus, how much weight does he put on the dating of the conquest? | He says the conquest dating confirms his Exodus dating, with a sequence from the Exodus through the initial conquest and Shiloh fitting the late-date model. | He says conquest dating confirms rather than independently recalibrates his Exodus dating. He sketches a sequence from an Exodus around 1265 or 1263, forty years to roughly 1223, about five years of initial conquest around Merneptah's early years, and then a period at Shiloh before the tribal inheritances. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6638" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:38</a> | Is gluttony the first sin? | No; he says gluttony is not mere overeating but taking someone else's rightful share, which is why the ancient world treated it so seriously. | He defines gluttony as eating another person's rightful share in an agrarian food economy, not simply eating too much. Because that theft can leave someone else starving, he treats gluttony as akin to theft and murder, but says it is not the first sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6893" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:53</a> | What tips does he have for writing an alternate-world fantasy novel with an 18th- or 19th-century archaeology subplot? | He says early archaeology was more like treasure hunting, so writers should read old field reports and capture the period's museum focus and problematic attitudes toward locals. | He says archaeology in that period was closer to treasure hunting than modern methodical excavation, with emphasis on spectacular museum pieces. He recommends reading old field reports from figures such as Petrie and others to capture their mindset, including the real historical problems in how remains, local people, and artifacts were treated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=7408" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:28</a> | Has he made his own Bible timeline, and can viewers see it? | Not a full one yet; he says he is still working on a peer-reviewed patriarchal timeline and does not want to release it prematurely. | He says he does not have a complete Bible timeline to share. He is working on a peer-reviewed article about the patriarchal timeline and thinks the findings are important enough not to release casually before publication. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=7477" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:37</a> | Is Ezekiel 29 a failed prophecy about Egypt? | He says no; he reads the passage as promising Nebuchadnezzar loot from Egypt as wages for his army, not permanent conquest of the land. | He focuses on Ezekiel 29:18-19 and says the promise is that Nebuchadnezzar will plunder Egypt as wages after receiving no spoil from Tyre. He argues the text promises loot, not permanent rule over Egypt, and he connects later drops in Egyptian sacred furniture quality with episodes of conquest or plundering, possibly including Babylonian activity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=7815" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:15</a> | Has he heard of a book claiming stylometry proves the New Testament is second century and Paul did not exist? | He has not read the book, but he rejects stylometry as junk science and says Paul's existence and first-century writings are not credibly denied. | He says he has not heard of or read the book, so he will not review it directly. Still, he rejects stylometry as junk science and says the claim that Paul did not exist or that his letters are second century is not credible, since Paul is quoted by early Christian writers before the second century. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=7994" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:14</a> | Would he consider academic cage-match style debate collaborations after the Faulk versus Kandy exchange? | No; he does not like doing debates and would rather teach and write than spend time on debate showmanship. | He says he might be good at debates but does not enjoy them and does not think they prove much, because viewers usually decide their side won. He prefers teaching, writing, and helping people become familiar with evidence rather than using time on debate showmanship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=8078" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:38</a> | Is the name Nimrod related or cognate to Ninurta, and could that fit a literary amalgamation? | He says Nimrod is clearly a literary amalgamation, but he is doubtful about deriving the name from Ninurta because the consonant shift seems unlikely. | He says the Nimrod figure is clearly a literary amalgamation because the cities and deeds attributed to him belong to different kings and periods. On the name itself he is cautious, but he doubts a derivation from Ninurta because an internal N-to-M consonant shift is not likely in the way final consonant shifts can be. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=8167" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:07</a> | Are literary amalgamations common? | Yes; he says they are common in Israelite literature and gives examples from both the Old and New Testaments. | He says literary amalgamations are common in Israelite literature, naming Zechariah in the New Testament and the Exodus oppression pharaoh as examples. He also uses the question to clarify that final M/N shifts in Semitic languages are more plausible than shifts inside a word, which bears on the Nimrod/Ninurta discussion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=8396" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:56</a> | In the offering of Isaac, is there a burnt-offering aspect to Christ's sacrifice, and could the fire be the wrath of God? | He warns not to make typology walk on all fours; the ram can be a type of Christ without every detail, such as the fire, mapping onto Christ. | He says typology works by analogy, not by making every feature of the type match the antitype. The ram can point to Christ, but that does not mean the fire, altar, or every sacrificial detail must correspond to some element of Christ's passion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=8640" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:00</a> | How can a homeschool co-op be persuaded to allow a logic fallacies and debate class? | He thinks the deeper problem is educational quality and fear of qualified teachers, and suggests outside evaluation or accreditation-style review rather than only arguing for one class. | He says the refusal sounds like a deeper co-op problem, not merely resistance to one proposed class. He suspects fear of qualifications, poor educational oversight, and internal politics, and suggests the only real route may be outside-style evaluation of teaching quality and curriculum, while acknowledging it may be a difficult or unwinnable situation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=9849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:09</a> | Why is God pictured as an old gray-haired man when the ideal age is 25 to 35? | He says this is an artistic way to convey God's title "Ancient of Days" and divine antiquity, not a claim that God is literally an old man. | He says art depicts God as an old gray-haired man because "Ancient of Days" requires a visual symbol of antiquity in anthropomorphic art. The image is not meant to say God is literally male, elderly, or weak, but to communicate divine ancientness as best the artist can. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=10066" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:46</a> | What did Ramesses II do after the Exodus? | He says Ramesses continued reigning for decades, completed little more building, lost many sons over time, and saw Egypt's power begin to weaken. | He says Ramesses II simply resumed reigning for many more years after the Exodus. Over time crown princes died, many sons were gone by the time Merneptah inherited, the chariot army was no longer what it had been, Philistines occupied the coastal plain, and Egypt showed serious cracks by Merneptah's early reign. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=10368" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:48</a> | Why did the Egyptians not begin to worship Yahweh after the Exodus if their gods could not stop him? | He says adopting Yahweh would have required a deep metaphysical shift from polytheism to monotheism, and Egyptians may also have seen Yahweh as leaving with Israel. | He says Yahweh worship is not simply swapping one god for another; it requires moving from many limited, local gods to one transcendent God of the universe. That metaphysical shift is too large to happen automatically, and he also notes there was no formal Egyptian cult of Yahweh established after the Exodus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=10704" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:24</a> | Does the Bible say whether non-delta Egyptian polytheists knew where or from whom the ten plagues originated? | He says the Bible does not say, and upper Egyptians may have experienced some plagues, such as the Nile turning to blood, without knowing all their origin. | He says upper Egyptians would likely have noticed some plagues, especially the Nile turning to blood, but may not have known where the events originated. Because politics, diplomacy, and much attention were centered in the Delta in this period, and some plagues may not have affected Upper Egypt in the same way, the Bible leaves their awareness unclear. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=10828" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:28</a> | Did the Egyptians' metaphysical framework radically change when they became Christian? | He says the framework had already been shifting through later Egyptian religion, Greco-Roman influence, and Jewish presence before Christianity arrived. | He says the framework did not suddenly change only at conversion to Christianity; the groundwork had been laid earlier. Later Egyptian religion developed ideas of personal sin and afterlife punishment, Greco-Roman and Levantine ideas entered Egypt, and Jewish presence exposed Egyptians to radical monotheism before Christianity arrived. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=10999" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:19</a> | Why did God use plagues and disasters against the king of Egypt? | He says the plagues were meant to get the people's attention and show Egypt was on the wrong course, while also pressuring the king. | He says the plagues were aimed at the people as much as or more than at Pharaoh, signaling that Egypt had strayed and was morally off course. The suffering of the people then put pressure on the king, who proved unable to fix the crisis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=11166" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:06</a> | Besides Kenyon's Jericho excavation volumes, what other books are worth searching for on archive.org? | He does not list specific titles, but recommends using Egypt Exploration Foundation resources and notes that archive.org has many older Egyptology and archaeology works. | He says archive.org has many useful older Egyptology and archaeology books, too many to list in the livestream. His practical recommendation is to use Egypt Exploration Foundation resources or mailing-list/newsletter notices that highlight freely available Egyptology and archaeology publications. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=11248" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:28</a> | Can someone be sued for calling another person a liar? | He says not usually for a vague accusation, but a false, specific, malicious, and damaging claim can become a defamation issue; he notes he is not giving legal advice. | He says the vague insult "liar" is usually too general for a suit, but a specific false accusation made maliciously and causing reputational or monetary damage may create defamation grounds. He stresses he is not a lawyer and adds that defamation suits are difficult, risky, and often not worth the cost. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=11381" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:41</a> | What is God's relationship to the material, and would anything existing alongside God be ontologically equal to God? | He says God must be transcendent from creation; otherwise monotheism collapses into pantheism or panentheism. | He says God must be outside and transcendent from the material creation. If God exists within the material order in the same way created things do, then God and creation become inseparable and monotheism collapses into pantheism or panentheism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=11551" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:31</a> | Was Ian Gillan correct when he said there is no laughing in heaven? | No; he says heaven includes joy, happiness, and humor, even if not spiteful mockery. | He rejects the idea that laughter is absent from heaven and notes that some medieval Christian traditions treated mature holiness as humorless. He argues Scripture and Christian tradition contain humor, including cheeky biblical moments, so heaven should not be imagined as joy without laughter. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=11942" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:02</a> | Is free will only for this world, with no free choice to sin in heaven? | He says people do not lose free will in heaven; rather, they know the cost of sin and will not choose it. | He says free will remains in heaven, but is exercised in its perfected state. People could choose sin in the abstract, as one could choose to harm oneself, but they will not because they know the cost of sin and live in the fullness of Christ's joy and love. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=12043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:43</a> | Does sin require a corporeal framework that is absent in heaven? | No; he points to fallen spiritual beings as examples that sin is not limited to corporeal existence. | He says sin is not limited to embodied creatures because fallen spiritual beings sinned without a corporeal framework. In heaven, redeemed humans will not sin not because sin is metaphysically impossible without flesh, but because they will know its wages and it will hold no appeal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=12175" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:55</a> | What would he do if Canada passed a law allowing arrests over religious beliefs? | He says the bill in question had been tabled, and if such a law passed he would keep doing the livestream and let the consequences fall where they may. | He says the discussed Canadian bill had been tabled or shelved for that parliamentary session, though it had sought to remove some religious-belief protections from hate-speech exemptions. If such a law passed, he says he would keep doing the livestream, continue saying what he says, and accept the consequences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=12530" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:50</a> | Was Chalcedon wrong to excommunicate the miaphysites? | He says that is somewhat anachronistic because the category was not yet developed; Coptic representatives may have sounded monophysite then, even though later theology was refined. | He says the question is anachronistic because "miaphysite" was not yet the operative category at Chalcedon, where the main categories were Nestorian and monophysite. He distinguishes Nestorian two-person Christology, monophysite confusion of attributes, and the later miaphysite middle position, then says the Coptic representatives may have sounded monophysite even if later Coptic theology was more refined. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=13132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:52</a> | Why do many Copts and Middle Eastern Christians seem complacent with Islam? | He says they are a minority under real social pressure and often must coexist carefully, though some still evangelize within legal and personal risks. | He says Egyptian Christians are a small minority among a large Muslim majority and periodically face church burnings and other pressure, so coexistence often requires caution. Some Copts do evangelize, but they must do it respectfully and often indirectly through common ground such as Old Testament prophets because of legal and personal risks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=13249" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:49</a> | Can Copts rebuild churches in Egypt if classical Sharia would restrict rebuilding places of worship? | He says Egypt is not governed by Sharia in that way, so Copts can rebuild churches, though they may not receive much protection from attacks. | He says Egypt is not governed by classical Sharia in that respect, so Copts are allowed to rebuild churches after attacks. He notes that many Coptic churches have rebuilding funds for this reason, but also says they should not expect strong protection from the government if churches are attacked. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=13740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:00</a> | Do Copts use Sahidic, Bohairic, or another dialect as their liturgical language? | He is not sure and thinks it may be Sahidic, but explicitly says not to quote him on that. | He explicitly says he is not sure and only thinks the liturgical dialect may be Sahidic. Because he cautions the audience not to quote him on it, the expanded answer remains limited to that uncertainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=13803" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:03</a> | Are there Christian converts in Egypt? | Yes, but he says conversion from Islam remains legally fraught, so converts often stay quiet about it. | He says there are Christian converts in Egypt, but conversion from Islam remains technically illegal or legally contradictory despite general claims of religious freedom. Because of that tension, converts often keep quiet about their conversion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=13920" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:00</a> | Why do people think finding the Ark of the Covenant would force people to admit God is real rather than just prove Hebrews built a ritual box? | He says finding it would not force faith; radical skepticism can always narrow the evidence and explain it away. | He says finding the Ark would at most prove that Hebrews built a ritual box, and possibly date it if it could be tested. It would not force belief in God, because radical skepticism can always narrow the accepted evidence or invent alternative explanations; faith requires more than seeing a gold-covered object. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=14173" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:13</a> | In light of Ephesians 2:14-16, is it difficult to eliminate pagan elements in church history from the Gentiles? | He says not inherently; pagan accretions should be discerned case by case, since some are harmful, some harmless, and some may even support the faith. | He reads Ephesians 2 as describing Christ making Jew and Gentile one obedient body. Pagan or cultural accretions in church history should therefore be evaluated rather than automatically retained or rejected: some are not foundational, some may distract from Christ, and others may be harmless or even helpful. |
